content:
2 min read
Just now

--

Two months back, Robert C. Martin (Uncle Bob) posted he doesn’t read the code his agents write anymore. He measures it instead: test coverage, dependency structure, cyclomatic complexity, mutation testing. The code itself he leaves to the AI, because to get any real productivity we need to “disengage from code and manage from a higher level.”
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Uncle Bob post on XComing from the Clean Code guy, that should’ve felt like heresy. It didn’t. If anything it made something click.
I build complex web applications for a living. Most of my week goes into planning and structuring things so they hold up: resilient, performant and secure, in other words engineering. The actual writing and reviewing of code already isn’t where most of my hours go, even before the LLM’s boom. So when Bob stepped off the craftsmanship hill, it didn’t shock me. It just named something that had quietly already happened.
So don’t panic about being replaced by an LLM. Its fully adoption part is coming for the coding, and honestly that part’s inevitable. But engineering was never really about writing code. If you’re already doing the real job, you’re already doing the part it can’t completly replace in the real world.
Coding is producing source. You take an intent and turn it into syntax that runs. Engineering is all the stuff around that: deciding what should even exist, where the boundaries go, what “correct” means for this particular system, and how you’d ever know you got there. We lumped the two under one name only because, for decades, the same person had to do both.
The agent is great at the first part. Fast, usually right. But the leverage didn’t go away, it moved up: the judgment, the structure, the taste, that was the job all along.
Which is exactly where Bob gets misread. “Leave the code to the AI” sounds like nobody’s watching it anymore. But that’s not what he’s doing. He didn’t stop checking the code. He changed what he checks it with. He swapped reading for measuring: coverage, complexity, dependency direction, the fingerprints of code that’s actually built well, and you can read those without reading every line. Same bar he always held us to, same suspicion he always brought, just a different instrument. The craftsmanship didn’t go soft. It moved up the stack with everything else.
Should we be happy about it? Mostly, yes. The tedious part is the part getting handed off. Just don’t cheer yourself all the way into never touching code again, because you can’t measure what you can’t read.
Step back from code as your default mode of production, sure. Just stay fluent enough that nobody can fool you.
Coding was never the job. The engineering is.
